title: Annotations Basics
description: Metadata attached to code with @ -- fully explaining @Override from the Polymorphism lesson, plus a few other common built-in annotations.
objectives: Understand an annotation as metadata attached to code, not executable logic itself
Fully explain @Override, previously used as a preview
Recognize @Deprecated and @SuppressWarnings as other common built-in annotations

sections.text: We've written @Override many times since Inheritance and Polymorphism, always as a fixed pattern. Today we explain exactly what it -- and annotations generally -- actually are.
Metadata attached to code (a class, method, or field) using @Name syntax, providing information to the compiler or tools without being executable code itself.

An annotation does not run like a statement -- it doesn't DO anything at the moment your program executes. It's information ABOUT the code, read by the compiler or other tools, similar in spirit to a comment, but one the compiler can actually understand and act on.

@Override tells the compiler: "I intend for this method to override a method from the superclass." If no matching method actually exists to override in the superclass -- often due to a typo like makeSond() -- the compiler produces an error immediately, instead of silently creating an unrelated new method, as we first mentioned in the Polymorphism lesson.
An annotation marking a method, class, or field as outdated and discouraged from further use, typically because a better alternative exists -- using it produces a compiler warning, not an error.
@Deprecated doesn't prevent oldAdd(...) from being called -- the code still compiles and runs. It only warns anyone using it that this method is on its way out and add(...) should be used instead.

@SuppressWarnings tells the compiler to hide a specific category of warning for the annotated code, when the developer has already reviewed it and is confident it's fine. It should be used sparingly and deliberately, not as a way to silence warnings without understanding them.

sections.title: Annotations at work here
points: @Override on Square's area() confirms it genuinely overrides Shape's area() -- caught by the compiler if it didn't
@Deprecated on Shape's oldArea() marks it as outdated, without preventing it from still working if called
Neither annotation changes what the program actually prints -- they're purely metadata for the compiler and future readers

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Thinking an annotation executes like a statement -- annotations are metadata, read by the compiler or tools, not executable logic themselves.
Skipping @Override out of laziness, and only discovering a typo'd method name at runtime instead of at compile time.
Assuming @Deprecated prevents code from compiling or running -- it only produces a warning, code still works.
Overusing @SuppressWarnings to silence warnings without understanding or fixing their underlying cause.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is an annotation in Java?
questions.answer: Metadata attached to code -- a class, method, or field -- using @Name syntax, providing information to the compiler or tools without itself being executable logic.

questions.question: What does @Override actually do, and why is it useful?
questions.answer: It tells the compiler this method is intended to override a superclass method. If no matching method exists to override (often due to a typo), the compiler produces an error immediately, rather than silently creating an unrelated new method.

questions.question: What is the difference between @Deprecated and a compile error?
questions.answer: @Deprecated only produces a compiler warning, marking something as outdated -- the annotated code still compiles and runs normally, unlike an actual compile error which prevents compilation entirely.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: An annotation is metadata attached to code using @Name syntax, read by the compiler or tools rather than executed as logic. @Override, used since Inheritance and Polymorphism, confirms a method genuinely overrides a superclass method, catching mistakes like typos at compile time instead of silently creating an unrelated method. @Deprecated marks outdated code with just a warning, not an error -- it still compiles and runs. @SuppressWarnings selectively silences specific compiler warnings when a developer has deliberately reviewed and accepted the underlying situation.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Annotation = a sticky note ON the code, for the compiler to read -- it never runs itself. @Override = "double-check this actually overrides something." @Deprecated = "still works, but please stop using this."

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: Does an annotation execute like a normal statement?
